---
title: "Swamp Rosemallow (Hibiscus grandiflorus)"
slug: "hibiscus-grandiflorus"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/474735467/medium.jpeg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "Swamp Rosemallow"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Magnoliopsida"
tax_order: "Malvales"
tax_family: "Malvaceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Magnoliopsida", "Malvales", "Malvaceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/474735467/medium.jpeg" alt="Swamp Rosemallow" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/justin48524">
justin48524</a> (CC BY-NC) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/264287346">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Magnoliopsida
-   **Order:** Malvales
-   **Family:** Malvaceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=163766)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not Listed
-   **Rarity note:** Rare species in Florida found in moist to wet habitats.

## Natural history notes

A large, showy wildflower characterized by its broad, soft leaves and very large pink flowers with a darker center. In Florida, it is found in moist to wet habitats such as marshes, swamps, and along pond margins. It blooms during the warmer months and provides nectar for pollinators, while its tall growth adds structure to wetland plant communities.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/163766-Hibiscus-grandiflorus)
-   [Local profile](https://www.flawildflowers.org/flower-friday-hibiscus-grandiflorus/)
